heroHeadline: Haiti TPS Bill Faces Senate as Trump Threatens Veto; GSF Deployment Continues, 90% of Capital Under Gang Control
heroSubtitle: The US House passed Haiti TPS extension legislation 224–204 on April 16, 2026 (10 Republican votes), but the Trump administration threatened a presidential veto, leaving 500,000+ Haitians in immigration limbo; Senate action remained unscheduled as of April 19. Haiti and the Dominican Republic confirmed Cap-Haïtien airport and three Dominican airports as the first routes to reopen in May 2026, ending a 2+ year airspace closure. Gang attacks in Seguin (Sud-Est) on April 13–14 displaced 4,000+ — the first large-scale gang displacement recorded in that department — while 25 deaths were confirmed in the April 11 Citadelle Laferrière stampede after Haiti's Culture Ministry fired officials and declared three days of national mourning. The UN reports 5.8 million Haitians face food crisis with only ~20% ($172M) of the $880M 2026 HRP funded. The Gang Suppression Force's advance contingent (~195 personnel) builds toward a 5,550-troop target by October 2026.
footerNote: Data sourced from UN BINUH, OHCHR, OCHA, IOM, Reuters, AP, ABC News, Miami Herald, Al Jazeera, France 24, Le Nouvelliste, ACLED, ICG, HRW, WFP, Haitian Times, Jamaica Observer, Jamaica Gleaner, RNDDH, PassBlue, Amnesty International, IMF, HaitiLibre, JURIST, International Rescue Committee, NPR, NBC News
